revision.text:
[[en:Abeno Shrine]][[fr:Abeno-jinja]][[ja:阿部野神社]] {{Short description|Shrine in Osaka Prefecture, Japan|qq=}} {{Infobox religious building|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | name = {{nihongo|'''Abeno Shrine'''|阿部野神社|qq=}} | image = File:Abeno-jinja-haiden1.jpg | caption = Abeno Shrine worship hall | map_type = Japan | map_alt = | coordinates = {{Coord|34.6283|N|135.5|E|region:JP_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} | map_relief = | map_size = | map_caption = | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | type = | deity ={{ill|Kitabatake Akiie|qid=Q1036864|1=Kitabatake Akiie}} | founded_by = | established = | date_destroyed = | location = | website = | architecture_style = | festival = | leadership = |religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|name = Abeno Shrine |location = 3-7-20 Kitabatake, Abeno Ward, Osaka City, Osaka Prefecture {{Infobox mapframe|zoom=12|type=point|qq=}} |ISO = JP-27 |Rank = 34|LatM = 37|LatS = 42 |longitude = 135|LongM = 29|LongS = 58.3 |mapCountryCode = JP |Status = Former Bekkaku Kanpeisha
Beppyo Shrine |established = 1882 (Meiji 15) |Pilgrimage = 44th station on the Shinbutsu Reijō Junpai no Michi (Osaka 3rd station) |festivals = January 24 |map = Osaka city | location = {{ill|Osaka|qid=Q35765|1=Osaka}} }} '''Abeno Shrine''' (阿部野神社, ''Abeno jinja'') is a [[Shinto]] shrine located in {{ill|Abeno-ku, Osaka|en|Abeno-ku, Osaka|qid=Q489009|lt=Abeno-ku, Osaka|1=Abeno-ku, Osaka}} Prefecture, Japan. Its main festival is held annually on January 24. It was founded in 1882, and enshrines the [[kami]] of [[Kitabatake Chikafusa]] and {{ill|Kitabatake Akiie|en|Kitabatake Akiie|qid=Q1036864|lt=Kitabatake Akiie|1=Kitabatake Akiie}}. It is one of the [[List of the Fifteen Shrines of the Kenmu Restoration|Fifteen Shrines of the Kenmu Restoration]]. ==Overview== [[File:Abeno-jinja.jpg|240px|thumb|Torii]] '''Abeno Shrine''' (あべのじんじゃ) is a {{ill|Shinto shrine|en|Shinto shrine|qid=Q845945|lt=Shinto shrine|1=Shinto shrine}} located in {{ill|Kitabatake|en|Kitabatake|qid=Q16871459|lt=Kitabatake|1=Kitabatake}}, [[Abeno Ward]], [[:en:Osaka City|Osaka City]], {{ill|Osaka Prefecture|en|Osaka Prefecture|qid=Q122723|lt=Osaka Prefecture|1=Osaka Prefecture}}. It enshrines {{ill|Kitabatake Akiie|en|Kitabatake Akiie|qid=Q1036864|lt=Kitabatake Akiie|1=Kitabatake Akiie}}, who fought for the [[Southern Court (Japan)|Southern Court]] across various regions, and his father, [[Kitabatake Chikafusa]]. It is one of the [[List of the Fifteen Shrines of the Kenmu Restoration|Fifteen Shrines of the Kenmu Restoration]], with the former [[shrine Ranking]] of Bekkaku Kanpeisha, and currently a [[Beppyo Shrine]] under the [[Association of Shinto Shrines]]. "Ab'''e'''no Shrine" is a misprint. == History == [[File:Abeno-jinja1.jpg|200px|thumb|Statue of Kitabatake Akiie]] The shrine's origins date back to 1875 (Meiji 8) when local volunteers built a small shrine to enshrine Kitabatake Akiie on the site where he is said to have been killed in the Battle of Ishizu against Kō no Moronao in 1338 (Engen 3). On February 27, 1878 (Meiji 11)"[http://jpimg.digital.archives.go.jp/pdf/S46B0300190000/1310.pdf#page=3 Selection of Abeno Shrine site in Sumiyoshi Village, Sumiyoshi District, Osaka Prefecture]" from ''Kōbunruishū'', Vol. 7, No. 62, p. 14., 15 individuals including Tojō Tokio and Matsumoto SomonAmong the petitioners, the first, Tojō Tokio, is the author of ''Kōtōdenryaku'' and ''Tōsetsu Jinja Ichiran''. Toda Genjo is the chief priest of [[Ikukunitama Shrine|Ikutama Shrine]]. Miwa Shun-nosuke distinguished himself in the {{ill|Boshin War|en|Boshin War|qid=Q309184|lt=Boshin War|1=Boshin War}} and later wrote ''Heiji Hikkei''. Fukui Takeshi later became the district governor of [[Shima District]]. Yamada Michikichi proposed the "Establishment of a National General Sammin Company" in the same year (included in ''Petitions and Memorials - Book of Petitions - 1875'' in the National Archives of Japan). applied to repair Kitabatake Akiie's grave in Abeno Village, Higashinarigun, and to build a shrine hall. The Osaka Prefecture required detailed plans for the buildings and grounds, a maintenance plan post-construction, and a detailed map separating the shrine area from the burial site, then asked for a re-application. The petitioners prepared 400 yen and planned to purchase land within Tennōji Village. After reapplying, the Osaka Prefecture forwarded the request to the {{ill|Ministry of Home Affairs (Japan)|en|Ministry of Home Affairs (Japan)|qid=Q954350|lt=Ministry of Home Affairs|1=Ministry of Home Affairs (Japan)}}, which agreed to the establishment of the shrine, with the Ministry deciding that the government should build a memorial at the burial site. This decision was approved by the [[:en:Dajōkan|Dajōkan]] (Council of State) on September 21. However, there were more twists and turns before the shrine was built. Osaka Prefecture suggested that Kitabatake Akiie should be treated with the same respect as other notable figures such as [[Kusunoki Masashige]], [[Nitta Yoshisada]], {{ill|Nawa Nagatoshi|en|Nawa Nagatoshi|qid=Q11415325|lt=Nawa Nagatoshi|1=Nawa Nagatoshi}}, and {{ill|Kikuchi clan|en|Kikuchi clan|qid=Q2975292|lt=Kikuchi clan|1=Kikuchi clan}}, and petitioned the Ministry of Home Affairs for appropriate shrine status. In November 1881 (Meiji 14), the Ministry decided to establish Abeno Shrine in Abeno Village as a Bekkaku Kanpeisha to enshrine Kitabatake Chikafusa and Kitabatake Akiie. This decision was formally approved by the Dajōkan on January 24, 1882. The plan to build a memorial at the burial site was abandoned."[http://jpimg.digital.archives.go.jp/pdf/S46B0300080000/0522.pdf Establishment of Abeno Shrine in Osaka Prefecture and Yūki Shrine in Mie Prefecture as Bekkaku Kanpeisha]" from ''Kōbunruishū'', Vol. 6, No. 59, p. 5.. Meanwhile, local volunteers in Osaka were collecting funds and had started purchasing land in Tennōji Village's Tenka Chaya for the shrine's construction. Learning of the decision to build in Abeno Village, 12 individuals led by Tojō submitted a request on March 22 to the [[Governor of Osaka Prefecture]], Take-no-Sato Gōsaku, asking to be allowed to construct the shrine in Tennōji Village. The group later decided to move the site to a nearby hill due to the original site's proximity to houses and poor drainage. They submitted plans for this new location to the Governor on June 2. After inspection, the Governor agreed and submitted the change to the Ministry of Home Affairs. In 1883 (Meiji 16), the Ministry agreed to follow the people's wishes after interviewing the priest of Abeno Shrine in Tokyo. The Dajōkan approved the site change on March 7."[http://jpimg.digital.archives.go.jp/pdf/S46B0300190000/1310.pdf Changing the site of Bekkaku Kanpeisha Abeno Shrine in Sumiyoshi Village, Sumiyoshi District, Osaka Prefecture]" from ''Kōbunruishū'', Vol. 7, No. 62, p. 14. Mibe Yoneo, "History of the Foundation of Shrines and Shrines", pp. 49-50.. The shrine hall was completed in March 1887 (Meiji 20), and the enshrinement ceremony was held in March 1893 (Meiji 23).Mibe Yoneo, "History of the Foundation of Shrines and Shrines", p. 51.. However, the hall was completely destroyed in the first {{ill|Bombing of Osaka|en|Bombing of Osaka|qid=Q2814395|lt=Bombing of Osaka|1=Bombing of Osaka}} on March 13-14, 1945 (Shōwa 20). It was listed as a Beppyo Shrine by the [[Association of Shinto Shrines]] in 1948 (Shōwa 23). The current shrine hall was rebuilt in 1968 (Shōwa 43). === Timeline === * 1875 (Meiji 8) - A small shrine to Kitabatake Akiie is built. * 1878 (Meiji 11) ** February 27 - Volunteers led by Tojō Tokio apply to build a shrine for Akiie. ** March 18 - Osaka Prefecture requests detailed plans. ** June 21 - Volunteers submit detailed plans and funding arrangements. ** August 2 - Osaka Prefecture forwards the request to the Ministry of Home Affairs, requesting approval. ** September 16 - The Ministry of Home Affairs petitions the Dajōkan to approve the establishment. ** September 21 - The Dajōkan approves the establishment. ** September 29 - Approval is granted to the volunteers. ** November 20 - Osaka Prefecture requests the Ministry of Home Affairs to grant the shrine equal status with other notable figures. * 1880 (Meiji 13) October 1 - Approval is granted to start construction. * 1881 (Meiji 14) November 16 - The Ministry of Home Affairs petitions the Dajōkan to establish Abeno Shrine as a Bekkaku Kanpeisha. * 1882 (Meiji 15) ** January 24 - The Dajōkan orders the establishment of Abeno Shrine in Abeno Village as a Bekkaku Kanpeisha. ** March 22 - Volunteers led by Tojō apply to build the shrine in Tennōji Village. ** June 2 - Volunteers apply for another site change. ** September 22 - The Rank of Abeno Shrine among Bekkaku Kanpeisha is determined to follow [[Nawa Shrine]]."[http://jpimg.digital.archives.go.jp/pdf/S46B0300080000/0512.pdf Setting the Ranks of Kanpeichūsha Hie Shrine and Bekkaku Kanpeisha Abeno, Yūki, and Komikado Shrines]" from ''Kōbunruishū'', Vol. 6, No. 59, p. 2.. ** October 31 - Osaka Prefecture petitions the Ministry of Home Affairs to change the site to Yabuyama in Sumiyoshi Village. * 1883 (Meiji 16) ** February 1 - The Ministry of Home Affairs petitions the Dajōkan to grant the people's request. ** February 26 - The second bureau of the Dajōkan agrees with the Ministry of Home Affairs' petition. ** March 7 - The Dajōkan approves the site change to Yabuyama in Sumiyoshi Village. * 1887 (Meiji 20) March - The shrine hall is completed. * 1893 (Meiji 23) March - The enshrinement ceremony is held. * 1945 (Shōwa 20) March 13-14 - The shrine is completely destroyed in the first Bombing of Osaka. * 1968 (Shōwa 43) - The shrine hall is rebuilt. == Deities == * Main deities - [[Kitabatake Chikafusa]], {{ill|Kitabatake Akiie|en|Kitabatake Akiie|qid=Q1036864|lt=Kitabatake Akiie|1=Kitabatake Akiie}} == Song == * "[[Railway Song]] Vol. 5 Kansai, Sangu, Nankai Edition (1900)" (Lyrics: [[Ōwada Takeki]], Composition: [[Umetake Ō]]), mentions this area around Abeno Shrine (including [[Seimei Shrine]] in Abeno Ward) alongside {{ill|Orito|en|Orito|qid=Q12981826|lt=Orito-no|1=Orito}} in Sumiyoshi Ward. : 63. The evening storm in Orito-no blows through the pine shadows of Abeno. There is a shrine for Akiie and his father, but where is the trace of their loyal deaths? == Grounds == * Main hall - Rebuilt in 1968 (Shōwa 43). * Worship hall - Rebuilt in 1968 (Shōwa 43). * Kunomamiya - Deities: [[Nanbu Mori-yuki]] and his 108 retainers * Ancestral Spirit Shrine * Inner Shrine (Mitamafuri-no-Miya) - Deities: [[Amaterasu]], [[Ōmononushi|Miwa Ōkami]], [[Sukunabikona|Sukunahikona]], {{ill|Sugawara no Michizane|en|Sugawara no Michizane|qid=Q382005|lt=Sugawara no Michizane|1=Sugawara no Michizane}}. Located behind the main hall. * Flag-raising Inari Shrine - Deities: Toyouke Inari Ōkami, Takakura Daimyōjin, Shirataka Daimyōjin * Flag-raising Entertainment Inari Shrine - deity: [[Ōmiya-no-Me|Ōmiya-no-Me Ōkami]] (Amaterasu-Omikami) * Earth Shrine - deity: Shirahime Daimyōjin (Landowner deity) * Harai-no-Yashiro - deity: [[Haraedo no Kami|Haraedo no Ōkami]] * Shrine office * Butokuden (Martial Virtue Hall) * Statue of Kitabatake Akiie * Tea house "Nakaimatei" == Pilgrimage Route == ; [[Shinbutsu Reijō Junpai no Michi]] : 43 [[Shitennō-ji]] * '''44 Abeno Shrine''' - 45 [[Imamiya Ebisu Shrine]] == Transportation == * [[Hankai Tramway Hankai Line]] [[Tenjin-no-Mori Station]] ** Formerly, "Miyano-shita Station" was located just south of Tenjin-no-Mori Station, beneath the west gate of Abeno Shrine. It opened with the Hankai Line in 1911 as "Abeno Shrine Station" and was abolished before WWII. The platform remains today. * {{ill|Nankai Main Line|en|Nankai Main Line|qid=Q59453|lt=Nankai Main Line|1=Nankai Main Line}} and [[:en:Nankai Kōya Line|Kōya Line]] {{ill|Kishinosato-Tamade Station|en|Kishinosato-Tamade Station|qid=Q6416603|lt=Kishinosato-Tamade Station|1=Kishinosato-Tamade Station}} ** The former Kishi-no-sato Station opened in 1900 as "Katsuma Station" on the predecessor of the Nankai Kōya Line, and was renamed "Abeno Station" in 1903. When it merged with Nankai Railway in 1925, it was renamed "Kishi-no-sato Station", and in 1993 it merged with the former Tamade Station to become "Kishinosato-Tamade Station". == References == {{Reflist|qq=}} == Further Reading == *[https://dl.ndl.go.jp/pid/1112175/1/1 Kinnō Jiseiki Bekkaku Kanpeisha Seishi] by Yamada Yoshiyoshi, edited by Ni-Roku Kōshinjo, 1935 (National Diet Library Digital Collection) * Mibe Yoneo, "History of the Foundation of Shrines and Shrines", ''Meiji Restoration Shinto Centennial History'' Vol. 2, Shinto Cultural Association, 1966. * ''Daijōruiten'' from the National Archives of Japan [https://www.digital.archives.go.jp/ Digital Archive], accessed October 2011. * ''Kōbunruishū'' from the National Archives of Japan Digital Archive, accessed October 2011. == External Links == {{commons|Category:Abeno-jinja|qq=}} * [https://osaka-info.jp/page/abenojinja Abeno Shrine | OSAKA-INFO] {{shinto-stub|qq=}} ==See also== *[[List of the Fifteen Shrines of the Kenmu Restoration|Fifteen Shrines of the Kenmu Restoration]] {{Commons Category|Abeno-jinja|qq=}} ==External links== *[http://www.abenojinjya.com Official website] {{Shinto shrine|qq=}} {{Shinto-stub|qq=}} {{Beppyo Shrines|qq=}} {{Fifteen Shrines of the Kenmu Restoration|qq=}} {{translated page|ja|阿部野神社|version=104364016|comment=Imported full ja history}} [[en:Abeno Shrine]] [[ja:阿部野神社]] {{Commons category|Abeno-jinja}} [[fr:Abeno-jinja]] {{Commons category|Abeno-jinja}} [[fr:Abeno-jinja]] {{wikidata link|Q11657965}} [[Category:1880s establishments in Japan]] [[Category:Bekkaku Kanpeisha]] [[Category:Beppyo shrines]] [[Category:Kitabatake family]] [[Category:Kitabatake Chikafusa]] [[Category:Kitabatake Akiie]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Osaka]] [[Category:建武中興十五社]] [[Category:晴明丘・北畠]] [[Category:Pilgrimage Route of Shinto and Buddhist Sacred Sites]] [[Category:阿倍野区の建築物]] [[Category:阿倍野区の歴史]]
